cue: How do you say something went just okay?

es.meaning: Fine, but not great — the deflating follow-up.
es.example: —¿Cómo salió el examen? —Bien... pero no tan bien.
es.exampleTranslation: “How did the exam go?” “Fine... but not THAT fine.”
es.regionNote: PAIRING UNCERTAIN: Spanish has no fixed twin for the Italian phrase; this is the transparent construction. María to rule whether 'más o menos', 'regular', or MX 'ahí la llevo' serves better.

it.meaning: Fine, but not great — ironic understatement; things are slightly off.
it.exampleTranslation: “How did it go?” “Good... but not GREAT.”

bridge.note: Underneath the joke is a grammar asymmetry: Italian bolts -issimo onto almost anything, adverbs included — bene becomes benissimo, presto becomes prestissimo — while Spanish blocks it on bien (no *bienísimo in the standard; you escalate with muy bien or, in Mexico, rebién). Italian owns a one-word 'very well'; Spanish has to build it. The phrase runs on machinery the Spanish speaker doesn't expect to exist.

interference.es_to_it: Spanish speakers under-use -issimo because their own language rations -ísimo; in Italian it's everywhere and unmarked — benissimo, pochissimo, even jocular subitissimo.
interference.it_to_es: Italians generalize the machinery into Spanish and produce 'bienísimo' — a classic Italian-accent error; standard Spanish wants muy bien (colloquial MX: rebién).

review.notes: Submitted by Joe 2026-07-17. IT phrase is meme-boosted (mid-2010s) but now ordinary speech — IT reviewer to confirm the register aged well. ES pairing flagged. Arrow characters removed from the bridge note 2026-07-19: the serif italic renders them from a fallback face much wider than metrics predict, overflowing the Instagram panel. Prose reads better without them anyway.
